description: The provided image is unreadable due to extremely low resolution, appearing merely as a small circular icon resembling a bookmark or ribbon symbol without any discernible scientific data, axes, curves, or microstructural features. Based on the accompanying text extraction summary from the paper 'Tensile deformation behavior analysis of low density Fe–18Mn–10Al–xC steels', the figure likely presents mechanical property data for three alloy variants: Fe–18Mn–10Al–0.5C, Fe–18Mn–10Al–0.8C, and Fe–18Mn–10Al–1.2C steels. The context suggests the figure may display stress-strain curves, strain hardening rate plots, or Crussard-Jaoul (C-J) analysis diagrams illustrating tensile deformation behavior. According to the extracted data, these curves would show distinct hardening behaviors: two-stage strain hardening for 0.5C and 0.8C steels versus three-stage hardening for 1.2C steel, with mechanical properties including yield strengths ranging 614.9–735.1 MPa, ultimate tensile strengths 856.7–979.1 MPa, and elongations 32.8–56.2%. However, none of these scientific details, sample labels, or quantitative values are visually verifiable in the actual image.

This figure presents optical micrographs showing the microstructural evolution of Fe–18Mn–10Al–xC low-density steels with varying carbon concentrations. Subfigure (a) displays the duplex ferritic-austenitic microstructure of the 0.5C steel, consisting of 59.2% austenite (γ) and 40.8% ferrite (α) with elongated grain morphology. Subfigure (b) reveals the 0.8C steel microstructure, showing a reduced ferrite content of 15.2% and increased austenite fraction of 84.8%, with visible evidence of ordered phases (κ carbides and B2/DO3 domains) distributed within the matrix. Subfigure (c) exhibits a fully austenitic microstructure in the 1.2C steel, characterized by equiaxed grains with prominent annealing twins and uniform precipitation of κ carbides. All samples underwent identical processing: induction melting, forging at 1200°C, homogenization, hot rolling with 90% reduction, intermediate annealing at 1000°C, cold rolling to 1 mm, and final annealing at 1000°C followed by water quenching. The images demonstrate that increasing carbon content progressively stabilizes the austenite phase while reducing ferrite volume fraction, transitioning the microstructure from duplex to fully austenitic, which correlates with the observed mechanical property variations including yield strength increasing from 614.9 MPa to 735.1 MPa and elongation improving from 32.8% to 56.2%.

This figure presents a comprehensive transmission electron microscopy (TEM) investigation of Fe–18Mn–10Al–xC low-density steels with varying carbon contents (0.5C, 0.8C, and 1.2C). Panels (a) and (b) display the austenite (γ) and ferrite (α) phases in 0.5C steel, respectively, with selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns confirming the fcc and bcc structures without ordered precipitates. Panels (c)–(e) focus on 0.8C steel: (c) shows austenite containing κ-carbide precipitates with SAED superlattice reflections (e.g., 100) confirming the L'1₂ perovskite structure, (d) reveals deformation twins in the γ phase, and (e) presents a dark-field image of the α phase showing B2 ordered domains with fine DO₃ particles. Panel (f) shows a dark-field image of the γ phase in 1.2C steel, revealing uniformly distributed κ-carbides in the fully austenitic matrix. The SAED patterns across all subfigures provide crystallographic evidence of phase evolution from simple duplex structures to complex ordered microstructures with increasing carbon content, supporting the correlation between carbon-induced precipitation, ordered phase formation, and the observed strain hardening behaviors.

This figure presents the engineering stress–strain curves of Fe–18Mn–10Al–xC low-density steels with three distinct carbon contents: 0.5C (black curve), 0.8C (red curve), and 1.2C (green curve). The x-axis represents engineering strain ranging from 0.0 to 0.6, while the y-axis shows engineering stress in MPa (0–1000 MPa). The 0.5C steel exhibits the lowest ductility with a total elongation (TE) of approximately 32.8% and an ultimate tensile strength (UTS) of ~857 MPa, failing at a strain of ~0.33. The 0.8C steel achieves the highest UTS of ~979 MPa with a TE of ~38.4%, demonstrating superior strength but intermediate ductility. The 1.2C steel displays the highest ductility with a TE of ~56.2% and a UTS of ~947 MPa, maintaining strain hardening to the highest strain level (~0.56). The curves demonstrate that increasing carbon content from 0.5% to 1.2% progressively enhances total elongation, while the UTS initially increases to a maximum at 0.8% C then slightly decreases at 1.2% C. This supports the core conclusion that the 1.2C steel achieves an exceptional strength–ductility combination, with a UTS × TE product of 53.2 GPa%, nearly double that of the 0.5C steel (28.1 GPa%), attributable to the fully austenitic microstructure containing uniformly distributed κ carbides and sustained strain hardening capacity.

This figure presents the strain hardening rate (dσ/dε) versus true strain curves for Fe–18Mn–10Al–xC low-density steels with three different carbon contents: 0.5C (black curve), 0.8C (red curve), and 1.2C (green curve). The plot reveals distinctly different strain hardening behaviors among the three alloys. Both 0.5C and 0.8C steels exhibit a two-stage strain hardening characteristic with continuously decreasing strain hardening rates as strain increases, though 0.8C maintains higher strain hardening values than 0.5C throughout most of the deformation process. In contrast, the 1.2C steel displays a unique three-stage strain hardening behavior: an initial rapid decrease followed by an increasing stage reaching a plateau around 1900 MPa between 0.15–0.35 strain, and finally a sharp drop at high strains. The 1.2C steel sustains sufficient strain hardening capacity beyond 0.026 strain to prevent strain localization, correlating with its superior ductility (56.2% elongation) compared to 0.5C (32.8%) and 0.8C (38.4%) steels. This variation is attributed to microstructural differences: 0.5C has a duplex ferritic-austenitic structure without precipitates, 0.8C contains ordered phases (B2, DO3) in ferrite and κ carbides in austenite, while 1.2C is fully austenitic with uniformly distributed κ carbides, leading to different dislocation evolution mechanisms (planar slip, Taylor lattice, microbands) during tensile deformation.

This figure presents Crussard-Jaoul (C-J) analysis curves plotting ln(dσ/dε) versus ln(ε) for Fe-18Mn-10Al-xC low-density steels with varying carbon contents. Subfigure (a) shows 0.5C steel exhibiting two-stage strain hardening with a transition at ε=0.025, while subfigure (b) displays 0.8C steel with similar two-stage behavior but transition at ε=0.022 and a slightly steeper initial slope. Subfigure (c) reveals that 1.2C steel manifests distinct three-stage strain hardening with transitions at ε=0.026 and ε=0.137, fundamentally differing from the duplex ferritic-austenitic steels. The curves demonstrate that carbon content significantly influences strain hardening mechanisms: 0.5C and 0.8C steels show continuous decline in hardening rate attributed to planar slip in austenite and dislocation tangles in ferrite, whereas 1.2C fully austenitic steel sustains hardening through Taylor lattice and microband formation. The analysis indicates that ordered precipitates (κ carbides) in 0.8C steel accelerate the initial hardening decline compared to 0.5C steel, while the three-stage behavior in 1.2C steel reflects microstructural evolution from planar slip to microband interactions.

This figure presents six transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images illustrating the microstructural evolution of Fe–18Mn–10Al–0.5C steel during tensile deformation at different true strains. Images (a) and (b) capture the early deformation stage at ε=0.02, showing planar slip traces in austenite (γ) and dislocation tangles in ferrite (α), respectively. Images (c)–(f) display the microstructures at a higher strain of ε=0.18: (c) intensive dislocation slip in austenite, (d) Taylor lattice formation in austenite, (e) microbands in austenite, and (f) dense dislocation tangles in ferrite. The scale bars indicate 0.2 μm for images a–c, e–f, and 0.5 μm for image d. These micrographs provide direct evidence supporting the two-stage strain hardening behavior observed in this alloy, where the first stage involves planar slip initiation in austenite and dislocation tangle formation in ferrite, while the second stage is characterized by the development of low-energy Taylor lattice structures and microbands in austenite coupled with increased dislocation density in ferrite, resulting in sustained strain hardening capacity.
